flashcards.definition: A safety procedure ensuring dangerous machines are properly shut off and cannot be started up again prior to the completion of maintenance. Requires placing a lock and tag on the energy-isolating device.

NFPA 70 — a regionally adoptable standard for the safe installation of electrical wiring and equipment. Sets the foundation for electrical safety in residential, commercial, and industrial occupancies.
Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace. Addresses electrical safety requirements for employees including proper use of PPE based on arc flash hazard analysis.
Equipment worn to minimize exposure to hazards: safety glasses, gloves, hard hats, steel-toe boots, hearing protection, arc flash suits. Selection depends on the specific hazard.
A 16-section document containing information on potential hazards (health, fire, reactivity, environmental) and how to work safely with a chemical product. Formerly called MSDS.
Product identifier, manufacturer/supplier name and contact info, recommended use, and restrictions on use.
All hazards of the chemical and required label elements: signal words, hazard statements, pictograms, and precautionary statements.
Information on chemical ingredients including chemical name, common name, CAS number, and concentration.
Necessary first-aid instructions by route of exposure (inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion) and most important symptoms/effects.
Suitable extinguishing techniques and equipment, and chemical hazards that develop from fire.
Exposure limits, engineering controls, and personal protective equipment recommendations.
Safety barriers or devices on machinery to protect operators from rotating parts, flying chips, sparks. Required by OSHA for point of operation, nip points, and all rotating parts.
OSHA's 'Right to Know' standard requiring employers to inform employees about chemical hazards through labels, SDS sheets, and training programs.
Nine standardized symbols used on chemical labels: flame, exploding bomb, oxidizer, gas cylinder, corrosion, skull and crossbones, exclamation mark, health hazard, environment.
An area large enough to enter and work, has limited means of entry/exit, and is not designed for continuous occupancy (tanks, silos, vaults). Requires permit entry procedures.
Disconnecting machinery from ALL energy sources — electrical, mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, thermal, chemical — before servicing. Critical part of LOTO.
Stored energy that can cause harm: compressed springs, raised loads (gravity), pressurized systems, charged capacitors. Must be relieved before maintenance.
A safety device used to push material through cutting machines (table saw) while keeping hands a safe distance from the blade.
A: ordinary combustibles (wood, paper). B: flammable liquids. C: electrical fires. D: combustible metals. K: cooking oils/fats.
A multi-purpose dry chemical extinguisher effective on Class A, B, and C fires. Most common type in industrial settings.
OSHA requires proper housekeeping, adequate lighting, slip-resistant surfaces. Guardrails at 4+ feet. Ladders follow the 4:1 rule for setup angle.
For every 4 feet of working height, the ladder base should be 1 foot away from the wall. This ensures safe ladder angle (75.5°).
Do NOT touch the victim while they are in contact with the source. Disconnect power first, then call 911, perform CPR if needed.
Flush affected area with large amounts of water for at least 15-20 minutes. Remove contaminated clothing. Seek medical attention.
An explosive release of energy caused by an electrical arc. Can reach 35,000°F. Proper PPE (arc-rated clothing) and safe work practices per NFPA 70-E are essential.
A device that monitors current imbalance between hot and neutral wires. Trips in milliseconds if current leaks to ground, preventing electrocution. Required in wet locations.
Occupational Safety and Health Administration — federal agency that sets and enforces workplace safety standards. Employers must provide a workplace free from recognized hazards.
All hazardous waste must be properly labeled, stored in compatible containers, and disposed of through licensed facilities. Never pour chemicals down drains or into regular trash.
The science of designing the workplace to fit the worker. Reduces repetitive strain injuries, back problems, and fatigue through proper workstation design and tool selection.
Step-by-step documents describing how to perform a specific task. More detailed than SOPs and targeted at the operator level. Include safety warnings.
Documents recording equipment malfunctions: date, time, symptoms, corrective actions, parts replaced. Used for trend analysis and preventive maintenance planning.
Manuals and procedures describing how to start, run, adjust, and shut down equipment. Include safety warnings and normal operating parameters.
Recorded data from equipment testing: measurements, pass/fail results, dates, technician signatures. Used for quality assurance and compliance documentation.
Clear, concise verbal instructions using standard terminology. Critical during troubleshooting — technicians must accurately describe symptoms and relay findings to supervisors.
A documented plan for regular equipment inspections, cleaning, lubrication, and parts replacement to prevent unexpected breakdowns and extend equipment life.
Documented step-by-step instructions for routine operations. Ensure consistency, safety, and quality across all shifts and operators.
A document that records the problem, diagnostic steps taken, root cause identified, repair performed, and verification that the issue is resolved.
Measures electrical potential difference (voltage) between two points. Connected in PARALLEL with the circuit. Can measure AC or DC voltage.
Measures electrical current (amperes). Must be connected in SERIES with the circuit. Never connect directly across a voltage source — it can damage the meter.
Digital Multi-Meter — measures voltage (V), current (A), and resistance (Ω) in one device. Most common test instrument for mechatronics technicians. Input impedance typically 10 MΩ.
Measures resistance in ohms. Circuit must be de-energized and component isolated before measuring. Part of a standard multimeter.
Displays voltage waveforms over time on a screen. Used to analyze signal shape, frequency, amplitude, and timing relationships. Essential for troubleshooting AC and digital signals.
A precision tool applying specific rotational force (torque) to fasteners. Ensures bolts are tightened to manufacturer specs. Measured in ft-lbs or N·m. Click-type is most common.
Most common pressure gauge type. A curved metal tube straightens under pressure, moving a pointer through a mechanical linkage. Reads in PSI, bar, or kPa.
Turbine (spinning blades), Ultrasonic (sound waves), Magnetic (electromagnetic induction for conductive fluids), Vortex (measures turbulence frequency).
Converts pressure into an electrical signal (typically 4-20 mA or 0-10V). Types: strain gauge, piezoelectric, capacitive. Used for continuous monitoring in automated systems.
Temperature sensor made of two dissimilar metals joined at one end. Produces voltage proportional to temperature (Seebeck effect). Types: J, K, T, E — each for different ranges.
Temperature sensor where resistance changes with temperature. Made of platinum (Pt100 is most common). More accurate and stable than thermocouples but slower response.
A temperature-sensitive resistor. NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient): resistance decreases with heat. PTC: resistance increases with heat. Very sensitive but limited range.
Detects objects using light. Through-beam: separate emitter/receiver. Retroreflective: uses a reflector. Diffuse: emitter and receiver in one unit, detects reflected light from object.
Detects metal objects without physical contact using electromagnetic fields. Only works with ferrous and non-ferrous metals. Range typically 1-30mm.
Detects any material (metal, plastic, liquid, powder) using changes in capacitance. Useful for level detection through non-metallic container walls.
Magnetically activated switch with two thin metal reeds in a glass tube. Closes when a magnet approaches. Used for position sensing and counting.
A sensor that measures strain (deformation) of an object. Resistance changes when stretched or compressed. Used in load cells and pressure transducers. Typically in a Wheatstone bridge.
Measures shaft rotation. Incremental: outputs pulses per revolution. Absolute: outputs unique position code. Used for motor speed and position feedback in servo systems.
The resistance a measuring instrument presents to the circuit. High impedance (10 MΩ on DMM) ensures the meter doesn't significantly affect the circuit being measured (loading error).
Comparing instrument readings to a known standard and adjusting for accuracy. Should be performed on a regular schedule per manufacturer specs. Traceable to NIST standards.
Industrial standard for analog signal transmission. 4 mA = 0% (minimum), 20 mA = 100% (maximum). 4 mA baseline allows detection of broken wires (0 mA = fault).
The fundamental conversion between Imperial and Metric linear measurement. Also: 1 foot = 304.8 mm, 1 meter = 39.37 inches.
Precision instrument measuring to 0.001 inch or 0.01 mm. Uses a calibrated screw mechanism. Types: outside (OD), inside (ID), and depth micrometers.
Measures inside, outside, depth, and step dimensions. Vernier scale reads to 0.001 inch. Digital calipers provide direct readout. Range typically 0-6 inches.
Precision instrument measuring small linear distances (thousandths of an inch). Used for checking runout, alignment, and surface flatness. Magnetic base holds it in position.
Determines if a surface is perfectly horizontal. Bubble vial — centered bubble = level. Digital levels give precise angle readings.
Weighted tool on a string establishing true vertical reference. Gravity ensures the string hangs perfectly straight.
Versatile layout tool for checking 90° and 45° angles, measuring depth, and scribing lines. Has a ruler, square head, protractor head, and center head.
Any device converting one form of energy to another. In instrumentation: converts physical quantities (pressure, temp, light, position) into proportional electrical signals.
Accuracy: how close to the true value. Precision: how repeatable the measurement is. An instrument can be precise but not accurate (consistently wrong). Calibration fixes accuracy.
Electrical pressure/potential difference pushing current through a circuit. Measured in volts. Like water pressure in a pipe.
Flow of electrical charge through a conductor. Measured in amperes (amps). Like the flow rate of water through a pipe.
Opposition to current flow. Measured in ohms (Ω). Like a restriction in a pipe reducing water flow.
V = I × R. Voltage = Current × Resistance. Also: I = V/R and R = V/I. Power form: P = V×I = I²R = V²/R.
The sum of all voltages around any closed loop equals zero. Voltage supplied equals total voltage dropped across all components.
Total current entering a junction equals total current leaving. Current is conserved at any node in a circuit.
Components in a single path. Current is the SAME through all. Total R = R1+R2+R3... Voltage DIVIDES proportionally. One failure breaks entire circuit.
Components across the same two nodes. Voltage is the SAME across all branches. Total R is LESS than the smallest. 1/Rt = 1/R1 + 1/R2... Current DIVIDES.
A combination circuit with some components in series and others in parallel. Analyze by simplifying parallel sections first, then treat as series.
SI unit of electrical charge. 1 coulomb ≈ 6.24 × 10^18 electrons. When 1 coulomb passes a point in 1 second, the current is 1 ampere.
Unit of electrical power. P = V × I. Also P = I²R and P = V²/R. 1 horsepower = 746 watts.
Unit of energy. 1 kWh = using 1000 watts for 1 hour. This is what electric utilities bill for. Energy = Power × Time.
Black=0, Brown=1, Red=2, Orange=3, Yellow=4, Green=5, Blue=6, Violet=7, Gray=8, White=9. Band 1&2 = digits, Band 3 = multiplier, Band 4 = tolerance.
Gold = ±5%, Silver = ±10%, No band = ±20%. Tolerance indicates how close actual resistance is to the stated value.
Current periodically reverses direction. US standard: 120V at 60 Hz. Industrial: 208V, 480V 3-phase. Used for power transmission — voltage easily transformed.
Current flows in one direction only. From batteries, solar cells, and rectified AC. Used in electronics, PLCs, and control circuits.
Number of complete AC cycles per second. Measured in Hertz (Hz). US standard = 60 Hz. One complete cycle = 360 electrical degrees.
Transfers electrical energy between circuits through electromagnetic induction. Steps voltage up or down. Turns ratio: Vp/Vs = Np/Ns. Only works with AC.
Secondary has fewer turns than primary — reduces voltage, increases current. Example: 480V to 120V for control circuits.
Secondary has more turns than primary — increases voltage, reduces current. Used in power transmission to reduce line losses.
Converts AC to DC. Half-wave uses one diode (50% of cycle). Full-wave bridge uses four diodes (100% of cycle). Output is pulsating DC — smoothed by a capacitor.
Converts DC to AC. Variable Frequency Drive controls motor speed by varying output frequency and voltage. Saves energy, provides soft start, and precise speed control.
Converts mechanical energy to electrical energy through electromagnetic induction. Rotating conductor in a magnetic field produces voltage (Faraday's Law).
Standard control voltage in industrial automation. Steps down and rectifies line voltage for safe, low-voltage DC power for sensors, PLCs, and control devices.
An electrically operated switch. A small control current energizes a coil that creates a magnetic field, pulling contacts closed (or open). Provides electrical isolation between circuits.
A heavy-duty relay designed for switching high-current loads like motors. Rated for higher voltage and current than standard relays. Has main contacts and auxiliary contacts.
A contactor combined with an overload relay. Provides motor control (start/stop) and protection against overcurrent conditions.
Protects motors from overheating due to excessive current. Types: thermal (bimetallic) and electronic. Trips when sustained current exceeds motor rating.
An overcurrent protection device that melts and opens the circuit when current exceeds its rating. One-time use — must be replaced after blowing.
A resettable overcurrent protection device. Trips open on overcurrent, can be reset to restore the circuit. More convenient than fuses for frequent operations.
Mechanical switch activated by physical contact. Detects presence, position, or end-of-travel. Has NO (normally open) and NC (normally closed) contacts.
Three-terminal variable resistor providing adjustable voltage output. Acts as a voltage divider. Used for volume controls, position feedback, setpoint adjustment.
Stores electrical energy in an electric field between two plates separated by a dielectric. Measured in Farads (F). Blocks DC, passes AC. Used for filtering and energy storage.
Stores electrical energy in a magnetic field when current flows through a coil. Measured in Henrys (H). Opposes changes in current. Used in filters and electromagnetic devices.
Semiconductor allowing current flow in one direction only. Forward biased = conducts (0.7V drop for silicon). Reverse biased = blocks. Used in rectifiers and protection circuits.
Light-Emitting Diode. A semiconductor that converts electrical energy directly to light. Low power, long life. Used as indicators in control panels and circuit boards.
Semiconductor device that amplifies or switches electronic signals. NPN and PNP types. Three terminals: base, collector, emitter. The basis of all digital electronics.
Motors use interaction between magnetic fields (from permanent magnets or electromagnets) to create rotational force. Current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force (Lorentz).
Current flowing through a conductor creates a magnetic field. Strength depends on current and number of coil turns. Principle behind relays, solenoids, motors, and generators.
An electromagnet with a movable iron core (plunger). When energized, the plunger is pulled in. Used in valves, door locks, and starters. Linear motion device.
Electrical schematic for control circuits. Power rails (vertical lines on each side) with circuit elements drawn as rungs (horizontal). Read top to bottom, left to right.
Shows electrical connections and functions using standardized symbols. Shows logical relationships, not physical layout.
Shows physical connections and layout — actual wire routing, terminal locations, and component placement. Used for installation and maintenance.
American Wire Gauge. LOWER number = THICKER wire = MORE current. 14 AWG = 15A, 12 AWG = 20A, 10 AWG = 30A, 8 AWG = 40A.
Red = positive (+), Black = negative (−), Green or bare copper = ground. In AC: Black = hot, White = neutral, Green = ground.
Connecting equipment frames and enclosures to earth ground for safety. Provides a low-resistance path for fault current, causing protective devices to trip quickly.
Three AC voltages 120° apart. More efficient for motors and heavy loads. Delta and Wye configurations. Common industrial voltages: 208V, 480V.
Requires a starting mechanism (capacitor or split-phase winding) because single-phase AC alone produces no starting torque. Used for residential/light commercial.
Self-starting — three phases naturally create a rotating magnetic field. More efficient, smoother operation than single-phase. Standard in industrial applications.
Ferrous = contains iron (steel, cast iron) — magnetic, prone to rust. Non-Ferrous = no iron (aluminum, copper, brass) — non-magnetic, corrosion resistant.
Iron alloy with minimum 10.5% chromium. Highly corrosion resistant. Types: 304 (general), 316 (marine/chemical). Used in food processing, medical, harsh environments.
Iron-carbon alloy. Low carbon (<0.3%): ductile, easy to weld. Medium (0.3-0.6%): stronger. High carbon (>0.6%): very hard but brittle. Most common structural material.
Lightweight, non-ferrous, excellent corrosion resistance, good thermal/electrical conductor. Density is 1/3 of steel. Used in aircraft, heat sinks, and lightweight structures.
Excellent electrical and thermal conductor. Ductile, corrosion resistant. Used for wiring, plumbing, and heat exchangers. Brass = copper + zinc. Bronze = copper + tin.
Thermoplastics: can be re-melted and reshaped (nylon, PVC, polyethylene). Thermosets: permanent shape after curing, cannot be re-melted (epoxy, phenolic, polyester).
Monitoring machine vibration to detect problems. High vibration indicates misalignment, imbalance, worn bearings, or loose components. Measured in velocity (in/sec) or acceleration (g).
OSHA limit: 90 dBA for 8-hour TWA. Controls: engineering (enclosures, barriers), administrative (job rotation, scheduling), PPE (earplugs rated NRR 25-33).
Contains hardened steel balls between inner and outer races. Handles radial and light thrust loads. Most common bearing type. Sizes standardized by ABMA.
Uses cylindrical rollers for heavy radial loads. Higher load capacity than ball bearings of same size. Cannot handle significant thrust loads.
Tapered rollers handle combined heavy radial AND thrust loads. Used in wheel hubs, gearboxes. Always installed in opposing pairs.
Self-aligning bearings with barrel-shaped rollers. Accommodate misalignment. Used where shaft deflection or misaligned housings are expected.
Long, thin rollers for applications with limited radial space. High load capacity relative to size. Common in automotive transmissions.
Simple bushings with sliding contact (no rolling elements). For low speed, high load, or oscillating motion. Require lubrication film. Made of bronze, babbit, or polymer.
#1: Contamination. #2: Improper lubrication. #3: Misalignment. #4: Overloading. #5: Improper installation. Regular inspection and proper maintenance prevents most failures.
Creates a film between surfaces to reduce friction, dissipate heat, and prevent corrosion. Grease: most bearings. Oil: high speed. Proper amount is critical — over-greasing causes heat.
Positioning two machine shafts so centerlines are collinear. Misalignment causes vibration, heat, bearing/seal failure. Methods: dial indicator (rim-face), laser alignment (most accurate).
Shaft centerlines intersect at an angle. Causes twice-per-revolution vibration in the axial direction.
Shaft centerlines are parallel but offset. Causes twice-per-revolution vibration in the radial direction.
Straight teeth parallel to the shaft axis. Most common gear type. Two meshing spurs rotate in OPPOSITE directions. Simple but can be noisy at high speed.
Teeth cut at an angle to the shaft. Quieter and smoother than spur gears, handle higher loads. Produce axial thrust — require thrust bearings.
Cone-shaped gears for shafts that intersect (usually 90°). Spiral bevel gears are smoother. Used in differentials and right-angle drives.
A screw (worm) meshes with a worm wheel. High reduction ratio in compact space. Self-locking in most configurations (cannot be back-driven). Quiet operation.
Driven teeth ÷ Driving teeth. Ratio 3:1 means output speed is 1/3 input but torque is 3× input. Speed and torque are inversely proportional.
RPM_out = RPM_in × (Teeth_driving / Teeth_driven). Example: 1800 RPM × (20/60) = 600 RPM output.
A gear placed between driver and driven gears. Reverses rotation direction without changing the overall speed ratio. Simply adds a mesh point.
RPM_driven = RPM_driver × (Diameter_driver / Diameter_driven). Small driving large = speed reduction & torque increase.
Most common belt type. V-shape wedges into pulley groove for better grip. Slight slip is normal (1-3%). Multiple belts for higher loads. Replace in matched sets.
Toothed belt meshing with toothed pulleys. Zero slip — maintains exact speed ratio. Used where precise timing is critical (engine timing, CNC machines).
Roller chain on sprockets. Positive drive (no slip). For heavy loads and harsh environments. Requires lubrication and periodic tension adjustment.
Rigid: no misalignment tolerance (flanged). Flexible: accommodates slight misalignment (jaw, gear, disc). Fluid: uses hydraulic fluid for smooth power transfer.
Belt: general material handling. Roller: gravity or powered for boxes/pallets. Chain: heavy items. Screw: bulk materials (auger). Pneumatic: powders/granules through tubes.
Grade 2: low carbon, general use. Grade 5: medium carbon, most common. Grade 8: alloy steel, high strength. Higher grade = more marks on head = stronger.
UNC (coarse): fast assembly, general purpose. UNF (fine): precision, vibration resistance. Metric: M + diameter × pitch (M10×1.5).
Rectangular keys fit into keyways on shafts and hubs to prevent rotation. Types: square, rectangular, Woodruff (half-moon). Transmit torque between shaft and component.
A screw that threads entirely into one part to secure it against another (usually shaft to hub). Types: flat, cone, cup, half-dog point.
Snap rings that fit into grooves on shafts (external) or bores (internal) to axially position components. Installed/removed with snap ring pliers.
Seals: precision-machined faces, low friction, low leakage. Packing: braided rope compressed by a gland follower, adjustable, allows slight leakage for cooling.
Shows internal features by cutting through the object. Crosshatching indicates cut solid material. Half-sections show both internal and external features.
2:1 = drawing is twice actual size (enlarged). 1:1 = full size. 1:2 = half actual size (reduced). Always noted in the title block.
Contains drawing information: part name, number, scale, material, tolerances, revision history, approval signatures, drawing date.
A system of symbols specifying allowable variations in form, profile, orientation, location, and runout. More precise than conventional tolerancing.
Oil: flows freely, good for high speed, cooling ability, enclosed systems. Grease: stays in place, good for slow speed, open bearings. Selection based on speed, load, temperature.
A fluid's resistance to flow. High viscosity = thick (honey). Low viscosity = thin (water). SAE grades rate oil viscosity. Must match application requirements.
Uses pressurized LIQUID (oil) to transmit force. Incompressible fluid provides precise control. Operates at high pressures (up to 5000+ PSI). Used for heavy-duty applications.
Uses compressed AIR to transmit force. Air is compressible — less precise than hydraulics. Operates 80-120 PSI typically. Cleaner, faster, easier to maintain.
Hydraulic: incompressible, more force, precise, oil can leak/contaminate. Pneumatic: compressible, less force, faster cycles, clean (air), spring-like action due to compressibility.
Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid transmits equally and undiminished in ALL directions. F1/A1 = F2/A2. Foundation of hydraulic force multiplication.
Force = Pressure × Area. A 2-inch diameter cylinder at 1000 PSI: Area = π(1)² = 3.14 in². Force = 1000 × 3.14 = 3140 lbs.
Limits maximum system pressure by opening to tank when set pressure is exceeded. THE most important safety device in any hydraulic system. Set 10-15% above working pressure.
Maintains a constant lower pressure in a branch circuit regardless of main system pressure.
Directs flow to a second actuator only after the first reaches a set pressure. Controls the ORDER of operations in multi-actuator circuits.
Controls fluid flow rate, which determines actuator SPEED. Needle valve is the most basic type. Pressure-compensated types maintain constant speed regardless of load changes.
Controls the PATH of fluid flow — directs actuator movement. Designated by positions and ways: 4/3 = 4 ports, 3 positions. Operated by solenoid, lever, or pilot pressure.
Allows flow in ONE direction only. Blocks reverse flow. Free-flow and spring-loaded types. Used to prevent backflow and maintain pressure.
Fluid pressure in ONE direction only (usually extend). Returns by spring force or gravity. One port. Simple and cost-effective.
Fluid pressure in BOTH directions — extends and retracts under power. Two ports. Most common type. Retract force is less (rod reduces area).
Converts hydraulic energy into continuous rotary motion. Types: gear, vane, piston. Like a pump in reverse — fluid in, rotation out.
External gear pump: two meshing gears trap fluid between teeth and housing. Simple, rugged, low cost. Fixed displacement. Most common hydraulic pump type.
Sliding vanes in a slotted rotor sweep fluid from inlet to outlet. Quieter than gear pumps. Can be variable displacement (adjustable eccentricity).
Pistons reciprocate in a cylinder block. Highest pressure and efficiency of all pump types. Axial or radial configuration. Variable displacement available.
A hydraulic pump generates flow (GPM). Pressure is a result of resistance to that flow in the system. No resistance = no pressure buildup. Load creates pressure.
Must be correct viscosity for the operating temperature range. Too thin: internal leakage, wear. Too thick: sluggish, hard starting, cavitation. Viscosity Index indicates temperature stability.
Filter-Regulator-Lubricator: conditions compressed air before pneumatic devices. Filter removes particles/moisture (5 micron typical). Regulator sets pressure. Lubricator adds oil mist.
Reciprocating (piston): most common for small shops. Rotary screw: continuous duty, industrial. Centrifugal: very high volume. All require receiver tanks and regular draining.
Circle = pump/motor. Rectangle = cylinder. Diamond = filter. Arrow through symbol = variable. Spring = return mechanism. Lines = conductors/pipes.
Removes contaminants causing wear and failure. Locations: suction line, pressure line, return line. Filter rating in microns (smaller = finer). Contamination is #1 cause of hydraulic failure.
Stores hydraulic energy under pressure. Types: bladder, piston, diaphragm. Uses: supplement pump flow during peak demand, absorb shock, maintain pressure.
Formation and collapse of vapor bubbles in pump inlet. Caused by restricted suction (dirty filter, small line, high viscosity). Sounds like gravel in the pump. Destroys pump internals.

flashcards.term: Hydraulic Hose vs. Tube vs. Pipe
flashcards.definition: Hose: flexible, for moving parts, pressure rated, annual inspection. Tube: semi-rigid, bendable, clean appearance. Pipe: rigid, permanent installations, threaded fittings.
Industrial computer that continuously monitors inputs, executes stored program logic, and controls outputs. Replaced hardwired relay logic. Main components: CPU, I/O modules, power supply.
Repetitive process: 1) Read all inputs, 2) Execute program logic top-to-bottom, 3) Update all outputs, 4) Housekeeping/communication. Typical scan: 1-25 milliseconds.
Interface between field devices and PLC. Convert sensor signals to logic levels. Types: discrete (on/off), analog (4-20mA, 0-10V). Examples: pushbuttons, limit switches, sensors.
Interface between PLC and field devices. Convert logic levels to signals that control devices. Types: relay, transistor (DC), triac (AC). Examples: motors, solenoids, indicator lights.
PLC programming language resembling relay ladder diagrams. Contacts (inputs) on the left, coils (outputs) on the right. Rungs evaluated top to bottom, left to right.
Output is TRUE only when ALL inputs are TRUE. Like switches in SERIES. Boolean: Y = A · B. Truth table: only 1·1 = 1.
Output is TRUE when ANY input is TRUE. Like switches in PARALLEL. Boolean: Y = A + B. Truth table: 0+0 = 0; all others = 1.
Inverts input. If input = 1, output = 0 and vice versa. Boolean: Y = Ā. In ladder logic, represented by a normally-closed contact.
AND followed by NOT. Output is FALSE only when ALL inputs are TRUE. Most versatile — any logic function can be built from NAND gates alone.
OR followed by NOT. Output is TRUE only when ALL inputs are FALSE.
Exclusive OR. Output is TRUE when inputs are DIFFERENT (one high, one low). Output FALSE when both inputs are the same.
A table showing ALL possible input combinations and their resulting outputs for a logic circuit. Used to define and verify digital logic behavior.
Base-2 system using only 0 and 1. Each position represents a power of 2. Binary 1010 = 8+0+2+0 = 10 decimal. Foundation of all digital systems.
Software for creating precision 2D drawings and 3D models. Allows easy modification, dimensioning, simulation, and sharing. Examples: AutoCAD, SolidWorks, Fusion 360.
User interface connecting operators to machines/processes. Displays real-time data, alarms, trends, and controls. Modern HMIs are touchscreen panels.
Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition — monitors/controls processes over large areas. Collects data from remote PLCs/RTUs. Provides centralized monitoring and historical trending.
Standard network cabling using twisted pairs. Cat5e: 1 Gbps. Cat6: 10 Gbps (short runs). RJ-45 connectors. Maximum run: 100 meters (328 feet).
Transmits data as pulses of light. Immune to EMI. Very high speed and long distance. Single-mode: long distance. Multi-mode: short distance. More expensive, requires special tools.
Single conductor surrounded by insulation and braided shield. Used for older networks (10Base2, 10Base5), cable TV, and some instrumentation.
Router: connects different networks, makes routing decisions. Switch: connects devices within a network, directs traffic. Hub: broadcasts to all (obsolete). Server: provides shared services.
Unique numerical address for each device on a network. IPv4: four octets (192.168.1.100). Subnet mask identifies network vs. host portions.
EtherNet/IP: Ethernet-based, real-time. Modbus: simple, serial or TCP. Profinet: Siemens standard. DeviceNet: CAN-based. Selection depends on speed, distance, and device compatibility.
Wireless: flexible, easy install, susceptible to EMI/interference. Hardwired: reliable, secure, higher install cost. Critical industrial systems typically use hardwired for safety.
A motor with feedback (encoder) for precise position and speed control. Components: servo motor, encoder, drive/amplifier, controller. Used for CNC, robotics, automation.
Rotates in discrete steps (1.8° typical = 200 steps/rev). Open-loop (no feedback). Precise positioning at low speeds. Used in 3D printers, CNC, and positioning systems.
Proportional-Integral-Derivative: a control algorithm for maintaining a setpoint. P: response to current error. I: response to accumulated error. D: response to rate of change.
Temperature: thermocouple, RTD, thermistor. Pressure: strain gauge, Bourdon tube. Proximity: inductive (metal), capacitive (any). Position: encoder, potentiometer, LVDT.
Closed-loop: uses feedback to compare actual vs. desired output and self-correct (thermostat). Open-loop: no feedback, output not measured (toaster timer).
Ratio = Number of Driven Teeth / Number of Driving Teeth. Ex: 60 driven / 20 driver = 3:1 ratio.
Output RPM = Input RPM x (Driving Teeth / Driven Teeth). Ex: 1200 RPM x (20 / 60) = 400 RPM output.
P = V x I (Power = Voltage x Current). Also P = I^2 x R, and P = V^2 / R. Measured in Watts.
Rt = R1 + R2 + R3... Total resistance is the sum of all individual resistors.
1/Rt = 1/R1 + 1/R2... Or for two resistors: (R1 x R2) / (R1 + R2). Total resistance is always less than the smallest parallel resistor.
F = P x A (Force = Pressure x Area). Use this to calculate hydraulic cylinder force. Pressure = Force / Area. Area = Force / Pressure.
Area = π x r^2. If diameter is 4 inches, radius is 2 inches. Area = 3.14159 x 2^2 = 12.56 square inches.
MA = Length of Effort Arm / Length of Resistance (Load) Arm. Determines the force multiplication of a lever.
Torque (T) = Force (F) x Distance (d). Usually measured in foot-pounds (ft-lb) or Newton-meters (Nm).
Using the 4:1 rule, base distance = Ladder Height to Support point / 4. Ex: 20 ft wall = 5 ft base distance.
Tolerance is the total allowable variation in a dimension. Upper Limit - Lower Limit. Ex: dimension 1.000 ± 0.005 has a tolerance of 0.010.
T = 1 / f. Period is the inverse of frequency. For 60 Hz AC, T = 1 / 60 = 0.0167 seconds (16.7 milliseconds).
RPM = (120 x Frequency) / Number of Poles. Ex: 4-pole motor at 60 Hz = (120 x 60) / 4 = 7200 / 4 = 1800 RPM.
Span = 16 mA. A 12 mA signal is exactly 50% of scale (because 4 mA is 0% and 4 + 8 = 12).
V_rms = V_peak x 0.707.  Root Mean Square is the effective heating value of an AC voltage compared to DC. US 120VAC is an RMS value.
Current divides across parallel branches. I_branch = V_source / R_branch. According to KCL, sum of branch currents = total current.
Voltage drops across series resistors proportional to their resistance. V_drop = I_total x R_specific. Sum of drops = supply voltage (KVL).
Blue represents the number 6 in standard resistor color coding.
An integrated circuit that contains all the functions of a central processing unit (CPU). Acts as the brain of PLCs and computers in automation.
A transducer that detects the presence or absence of light, often used to count objects on a conveyor or trigger safety stops.
Driven gear is larger than the driving gear. Lowers RPM but mathematically increases the output torque available.
Selecting proper bolts, nuts, and washers to secure components. T-slot nuts are common for aluminum extrusion. Lock washers or threadlocker prevent vibration loosening.
Must transmit power, lubricate moving parts, seal clearances, and dissipate heat. The most important characteristic is viscosity.
Pipes, tubes, and hoses that carry compressed air. Rigid pipe for main headers; flexible polyurethane tubing for moving actuators.
Hardwired systems provide reliable, immediate connection critical for emergency stops. Wireless allows remote monitoring and mobile tools but is susceptible to interference and latency.
Pressure control (relief, reducing), Flow control (needle), and Directional control (spool, poppet valves).
Measured in slugs (Imperial) or kilograms (Metric). Mass is constant; weight changes with gravity.

Use cylindrical, tapered, needle, or spherical rollers to handle higher load capacities than ball bearings, especially strong in radial loads.
A major engineering field that designs, constructs, and maintains the physical and naturally built environment (roads, bridges, dams).
A major engineering field dealing with the design, construction, and use of machines to solve problems.
A major engineering field dealing with the study and application of electricity, electronics, and electromagnetism.
A major engineering field concerned with the development of aircraft and spacecraft.
A sub-discipline of civil engineering focused on the framework and foundations of structures, ensuring they can withstand loads.
A method that generates a three-view drawing (top, front, side) by placing a plane between the observer and the object.
Skillful research, ethical behavior, and critical thinking.
Henry Ford's assembly line, Wright brothers' first flight, Thomas Edison's light bulb, Robert Goddard's liquid fuel rockets.
Pulley, Lever, Wheel and Axle, Wedge, Screw, Inclined Plane.
Open discussion, speaking clearly to the audience, active listening, and not reading directly from slides.
1. Identify the need, 2. Research/gather info, 3. Develop solutions, 4. Choose solution (analysis), 5. Construct prototype, 6. Test and evaluate.
Evaluating based on Efficiency, Cost, Safety, Functionality, Availability of materials, and Durability.
Turning (lathe) for cylindrical objects, Milling for flat/square objects, Casting (liquid into a mold), Welding (arc, spot, gas).
A defect where the cross-sectional strength is reduced due to a groove melted into the base metal adjacent to the weld toe.
A precision instrument used to measure inside and outside distances accurately.
Machine screws (use hex nuts), Wood screws (coarse thread, pointy), Sheet metal screws (pointed heads, sharp fine threads).
UNC = type, 1/4 = diameter, 20 = threads per inch, 2 = length, 2A = thread fit/class.
Controls quality using methods like Six Sigma (standardization array), international organizations, and manufacturing tolerance to lower costs and reduce execution time.
Setting reordering levels to attain materials on time without overstocking.
Ferrous: high weight, lower conductivity, easily corrodible, magnetic. Non-Ferrous: malleable, high conductivity.
A graphical display of data using bars of different heights, used for representing quantitative (numerical) data. Bar graphs represent qualitative data.
Convection (fluid flow, hot to cold), Radiation (through vacuum/no direct contact), Conduction (direct physical contact).
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of 1 pound of water by 1 degree Fahrenheit.
Pipe roughness, number of curves/bends, and viscosity affect resistance in a fluid system. Smoother interior and fewer curves reduce resistance.
Minimize heat flow. Examples include fiberglass and sealed windows (using a vacuum between panes).
Using knowledge and skill for the advancement of human welfare, being honest, impartial, and increasing the reputation of the profession.
The ability of a material to be stretched into a long, thinner shape (like a wire) without breaking.
Combines physical/life sciences and math to convert raw materials into valuable forms.
Pressing or squeezing a material into a desired shape (forging, rolling, stamping).
An engineer who verifies whether a prototype or product meets the design's quality and performance objectives.
Provide water storage and supply pressure (via gravity) to move water through the distribution system.
One gallon of water weighs approximately 8.3 pounds.
Metals that do not contain any iron. They are generally non-magnetic and highly resistant to corrosion (e.g., aluminum, copper, brass).
Mild steel is an example of a ferrous metal because it contains iron.
The maximum resistance a metal has to being pulled apart before it breaks.
Testing methods that do not destroy the part being tested, such as vibration analysis, x-ray, or ultrasonic testing.
The ability of a metal or material to withstand shock loading and absorb energy without fracturing.
Steel reinforcing bars (rebar) are added to concrete primarily to resist the force of tension, which concrete is naturally weak against.
0 psi. Gauge pressure ignores standard atmospheric pressure.
14.7 psi (or 1 atmosphere). This is absolute pressure.
A coil surrounding an iron core to which voltage has been applied.
A two-way valve has exactly 2 ports. A three-way pneumatic valve typically attaches ports A and B to the pneumatic cylinder.
A solenoid works on the principle of electromagnetic force. When energized, it typically actuates upon a ferrous material like steel.
For highest efficiency, directional control valves should be sized according to their flow coefficient (Cv) rather than simply matching pipe sizes.
A continuous varying signal, such as a 60 hertz sine wave measuring 120 volts on an oscilloscope.
The worst enemy of a pneumatic system is contamination (dirt, water, oil).
Fluid in a hydraulic system is considered non-compressible, which allows it to transfer massive forces rigidly.
If contaminants block a fluid system, the blockage most frequently occurs first in the suction side of the system's pump.
The most accurate machine commonly used to efficiently cut square metal steel tubing.
A tool whose best use is to recut or repair damaged external threads on a bolt or shaft.
When milling aluminum, the mill should generally be set at a high RPM speed compared to steel.
Original Equipment Manufacturer.
Combining two directional forces. E.g., A force of 10 lbs West and 25 lbs South yields a resultant force of approx 27 lbs SW.
When charging, they give off dangerous, explosive hydrogen and oxygen gases.
A measure of operable uptime. For a 365-day year with 20 days of scheduled downtime, the availability is 345 days.
The progression of a project displayed in chart form using horizontal bar graphs to indicate time and specific tasks.
1. Truss members have no mass. 2. Members are allowed to pivot. 3. Forces are in line with the member. 4. Forces at each node balance to zero.
Member in compression gets shorter and fatter (reaction force points towards the ends). Member in tension gets longer and skinnier (reaction force towards the center).
A plain bearing consisting of a tube or sleeve that supports a rotating shaft.
When installing a sintered bronze bearing, it is necessary to align the oil groove with the oil cup in the housing.
Typically described by four features: manufacturer's code, bore, outside diameter, and length.
A general rule of thumb is heavier oils or grease are recommended for high loads and low speeds.
The purpose of the groove is to spread oil uniformly over the surface of the bearing so it does not gall.
The best solution for dirt in a plain bearing is usually to remove the source of the dirt or use a different sealed type of bearing.
An indication of its load carrying ability in a plain bearing material. P = pressure, V = velocity.
A device that reduces friction between two surfaces while supporting a load.
A soft bearing alloy consisting primarily of tin, lead, and antimony.
In a plain bearing installation, it is the difference between the shaft diameter and the bearing inside bore.
On a plain bearing system, oil can be applied directly to components or into an oil cup attached directly to the bearing.
The four basic components are: bearing, bearing housing, journal, and lubricant.
The ability of the bearing to withstand repeated application of stress and strain.
An aid that lists faults, probable causes, and corrective actions.
The best way to install a plain bearing into a bearing housing ensuring even, steady pressure.
If you inhale phenolic fumes/resins, it usually results in trouble breathing and respiratory distress.
A Class C fire extinguisher should be used on an electrical fire.
Represents a three-dimensional object using several two-dimensional views of the object (front, top, side).
Usually expressed in decimal or fractional units.
Represents hydraulic fluid flow in a schematic diagram.
If bolts and nuts are not properly torqued, they wear out wildly quickly and can cause catastrophic failure.
A safety practice used to prevent equipment from being energized. Extremely vital when working on Electrical, Fluid Power, and Mechanical systems.
Provides info about handling, storage, first aid, stability, and reactivity. Must be provided by chemical manufacturers.
The format of technical title blocks is NOT the same from company to company. Formats vary wildly.
A regulation that provides specific guidelines and details for electrical safety in the workplace.
To prevent a person from being electrocuted by detecting dangerous ground faults.
Move an electrocuted person ONLY if in immediate danger. If actively attached to a live circuit, first SHUT OFF the circuit.
Includes slipping hazards, slippery floors, and cluttered walkways.
The specific section of a technical report which summarizes the report's overall contents.

A method used to install a bearing into a housing by shrinking or expanding races respectively.
In a bearing press kit, the specific lip helps to closely align the drift plate to the cup during tightening.
Using an arbor press to install a bearing on a shaft results in perfectly even pressure being applied to the bearing race.
The inner bore of a replacement bearing must be actively measured to affirm it will fit on the shaft.
The highest speed roller bearings available.
Must be adjusted because the adjusting determines the structural play between the two parts of the bearing.
When using the direct method of mounting tapered roller bearings, the end play must be checked because it will increase as the shaft heats up.
Two major methods used to pack a bearing with grease are utilizing a bearing packer and intricate hand packing.
The structural component that neatly separates the rollers in an antifriction bearing, allowing them to turn freely.
The specified method of mounting tapered roller bearings that carefully places the backs of the bearing cups together.
A method of firmly retaining a tapered roller bearing that presses tightly against the cup of the bearing.
When using the basic hammer and driver method to remove a roller bearing, the inner race must remain robustly supported.
A bearing should NEVER be heated to a temperature exceeding 120°C (250°F) unless explicitly defined by the bearing manufacturer.
A precision tool actively used with a push-puller to cleanly and safely remove bearings.
A bearing press kit actively includes a hollow sleeve which provides space for the bearing to seamlessly come out of the housing during removal.

The three specified widths to detail a tapered roller bearing are overall width, cone width, and precise cup width.

questions.question: What safety device should be used to feed material into a table saw?
questions.explanation: A push stick keeps hands safely away from the blade while feeding material through cutting machines.

questions.question: Hazardous waste containers must be:
questions.explanation: The HazCom standard requires all hazardous containers to be labeled with GHS-compliant labels.

questions.question: Lock-out/Tag-out ensures:
options: Machines are safely shut off before maintenance
questions.explanation: LOTO prevents unexpected machine startup during maintenance by locking energy-isolating devices.

questions.question: NFPA 70-E addresses:
questions.explanation: NFPA 70-E is the Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace, covering PPE selection, arc flash analysis, and safe work practices.

questions.question: A Class C fire extinguisher is used for:
questions.explanation: Class C is for electrical fires. Never use water on electrical fires — it conducts electricity.

questions.question: Which SDS section covers first-aid measures?
questions.explanation: Section 4 covers first-aid instructions by exposure route (inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion) and important symptoms.

questions.question: Energy isolation requires disconnecting:
questions.explanation: ALL energy sources must be isolated: electrical, mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, thermal, chemical, and gravitational.

questions.question: The 4:1 ladder rule means:
options: Base 1 foot out for every 4 feet of height
questions.explanation: For every 4 feet of working height, the ladder base should be 1 foot from the wall, creating a safe 75.5° angle.

questions.question: A GFCI protects against:
questions.explanation: A GFCI detects current imbalance (current leaking to ground through a person) and trips in milliseconds.

questions.question: Arc flash temperatures can reach:
questions.explanation: Arc flash can reach 35,000°F — hotter than the surface of the sun. Proper arc-rated PPE is essential per NFPA 70-E.

questions.question: Equipment failure logs are used for:
questions.explanation: Failure logs record symptoms, actions, and parts replaced — enabling trend analysis to prevent future breakdowns.

questions.question: Standard Operating Procedures ensure:
options: Consistency, safety, and quality across operators
questions.explanation: SOPs provide documented step-by-step instructions ensuring all operators perform tasks consistently and safely.

questions.question: A multimeter measures:
questions.explanation: A multimeter (DMM) measures voltage (V), current (A), and resistance (Ω) — the most versatile test instrument.

questions.question: A voltmeter is connected:
questions.explanation: Voltmeters are connected in PARALLEL across the component being measured to read the potential difference.

questions.question: An ammeter is connected:
questions.explanation: Ammeters must be in SERIES so all current flows through them. Parallel connection can damage the meter.

questions.question: A thermocouple operates on:
questions.explanation: The Seebeck effect — two dissimilar metals joined at one end produce voltage proportional to temperature difference.

questions.question: A reed sensor is activated by:
questions.explanation: Reed sensors have thin metal reeds sealed in glass that close when a magnet approaches — used for position sensing.

questions.question: 1 inch equals:
questions.explanation: 1 inch = 25.4 millimeters — the fundamental Imperial-to-Metric conversion.

questions.question: Calibration is:
options: Comparing to a known standard and adjusting
questions.explanation: Calibration compares readings to a known traceable standard and adjusts the instrument to ensure accuracy.

questions.question: High input impedance on a meter prevents:
questions.explanation: 10 MΩ input impedance means the meter draws negligible current, preventing measurement loading errors.

questions.question: A 4-20 mA signal uses 4 mA as the baseline because:
questions.explanation: Using 4 mA as the zero/minimum allows the system to distinguish between a 0% signal (4 mA) and a broken wire (0 mA).

questions.question: An inductive proximity sensor detects:
questions.explanation: Inductive proximity sensors use electromagnetic fields to detect metal objects. Capacitive sensors detect any material.

questions.question: A dial indicator measures in:
questions.explanation: Dial indicators measure in thousandths of an inch (0.001") and are used for checking runout, alignment, and flatness.

questions.question: Ohm's Law states:
questions.explanation: V = I × R: Voltage equals Current times Resistance. The fundamental relationship governing all electrical circuits.

questions.question: In a series circuit, if one component fails:
questions.explanation: Series circuits have one current path — if any component opens, the entire circuit is broken.

questions.question: In a parallel circuit, voltage across each branch is:
questions.explanation: All parallel branches connect across the same two nodes — voltage is identical across every branch.

questions.question: V=12V, R=4Ω. Current equals:
questions.explanation: I = V/R = 12V / 4Ω = 3 Amperes.

questions.question: Brown-Black-Red-Gold resistor value is:
questions.explanation: Brown=1, Black=0, Red=×100. Value = 10 × 100 = 1000Ω (1kΩ). Gold = ±5% tolerance.

questions.question: A transformer operates on:
questions.explanation: A changing magnetic field in the primary coil induces voltage in the secondary coil — only works with AC.

questions.question: Lower AWG number means:
questions.explanation: In AWG: lower number = thicker wire = greater current capacity. 10 AWG is thicker than 14 AWG.

questions.question: An inverter converts:
questions.explanation: An inverter converts DC to AC. VFDs use inverters to control motor speed by varying frequency and voltage.

questions.question: KVL states:
options: Total current at a junction = zero
Sum of voltages around a closed loop = zero
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law: the algebraic sum of all voltages around any closed loop equals zero.

questions.question: A relay provides:
options: Electrical isolation between control and load circuits
questions.explanation: Relays use a small control current to switch a larger load circuit, providing electrical isolation between the two.

questions.question: What creates a magnetic field?
questions.explanation: Current flowing through a conductor creates a magnetic field — the principle of electromagnetism.

questions.question: A ladder diagram reads:
options: Top to bottom, left to right
questions.explanation: Ladder diagrams are read top to bottom (rung sequence) and left to right (logic flow from input to output).

questions.question: The unit of electrical power is:
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts. P = V × I = I²R = V²/R. One horsepower = 746 watts.

questions.question: A diode allows current flow in:
questions.explanation: A diode is a one-way valve for electricity. It conducts when forward biased and blocks when reverse biased.

questions.question: A capacitor:
options: Stores energy in a magnetic field
Stores energy in an electric field
questions.explanation: Capacitors store energy in an electric field between two plates. They block DC and pass AC.

questions.question: 3-phase power uses how many AC voltages?
questions.explanation: Three-phase power consists of three AC voltages separated by 120 electrical degrees — more efficient for motors.

questions.question: Bearing failure cause #1 is:
questions.explanation: Contamination (dirt, moisture, debris) is the #1 cause of bearing failure. Proper sealing and clean lubrication are critical.

questions.question: Gear ratio with 20-tooth driver and 60-tooth driven:
questions.explanation: Ratio = Driven/Driver = 60/20 = 3:1. Output is 1/3 the speed but 3× the torque.

questions.question: Two meshing spur gears rotate:
questions.explanation: Meshing spur gears always rotate in opposite directions. An idler gear reverses this.

questions.question: Shaft misalignment causes:
questions.explanation: Misalignment causes vibration, heat, premature bearing/seal failure, and increased power consumption.

questions.question: V-belts grip better because:
options: The V-shape wedges into the pulley groove
questions.explanation: The V-shaped cross section wedges into the groove, creating more friction surface contact than flat belts.

questions.question: If input pulley is 4" at 1800 RPM and output is 12", output RPM is:
questions.explanation: RPM_out = RPM_in × (D_in/D_out) = 1800 × (4/12) = 600 RPM.

questions.question: Ferrous metals:
questions.explanation: Ferrous = iron-containing, magnetic, and prone to rust. Steel and cast iron are ferrous.

questions.question: A synchronous (timing) belt:
options: Has zero slip due to teeth
Is used only for light loads
questions.explanation: Toothed timing belts mesh with toothed pulleys for zero slip — used where precise timing is critical.

questions.question: Tapered roller bearings handle:
options: Combined heavy radial AND thrust loads
questions.explanation: Tapered rollers handle combined heavy radial and thrust loads. Always installed in opposing pairs.

questions.question: Needle bearings are best for:
questions.explanation: Needle bearings have long, thin rollers giving high load capacity in minimal radial space.

questions.question: A worm gear set is often:
questions.explanation: Most worm gear configurations are self-locking — the load cannot drive the worm backward. Used for hoists and elevators.

questions.question: UNF threads vs UNC threads:
options: UNF is finer with better vibration resistance
questions.explanation: UNF (fine) has more threads per inch — better vibration resistance and precision. UNC (coarse) is for general/fast assembly.

questions.question: Viscosity is:
questions.explanation: Viscosity measures how thick or thin a fluid flows. High viscosity = thick (honey), low viscosity = thin (water).

questions.question: A Grade 8 bolt vs Grade 5:
options: Is stronger — made from alloy steel
questions.explanation: Grade 8 is alloy steel, significantly stronger than Grade 5 medium carbon steel. More marks on head = higher grade.

questions.question: Pascal's Law states pressure in an enclosed fluid transmits:
questions.explanation: Pascal's Law: pressure transmits equally and undiminished in ALL directions throughout the enclosed fluid.

questions.question: Hydraulic uses liquid, pneumatic uses:
questions.explanation: Hydraulic = incompressible liquid (oil). Pneumatic = compressible gas (air). Different properties = different applications.

questions.question: A double-acting cylinder:
questions.explanation: Double-acting uses fluid pressure in both directions — extends AND retracts under power through two ports.

questions.question: A relief valve:
questions.explanation: The relief valve is the primary safety device — opens to tank when pressure exceeds the set limit.

questions.question: FRL stands for:
questions.explanation: Filter-Regulator-Lubricator conditions compressed air: removes particles/moisture, sets pressure, adds oil mist.

questions.question: Hydraulic pumps create:
questions.explanation: Pumps generate FLOW (GPM). Pressure results from resistance to flow. No load = no pressure buildup.

questions.question: Cavitation in a hydraulic pump:
options: Is vapor bubble formation from restricted suction
questions.explanation: Cavitation = vapor bubbles forming and collapsing at the inlet. Sounds like gravel and destroys pump internals.

questions.question: A flow control valve controls actuator:
questions.explanation: Flow rate determines actuator speed. Restricting flow slows movement; increasing flow speeds it up.

questions.question: System filtration is critical because:
options: Contamination is #1 cause of hydraulic failure
questions.explanation: Contamination causes wear to pumps, valves, and cylinders. Filter ratings in microns — lower = finer filtration.

questions.question: A check valve allows flow:
questions.explanation: Check valves are one-way valves — they allow flow in one direction and block reverse flow.

questions.question: A PLC replaces:
questions.explanation: PLCs replaced hardwired relay logic with flexible, programmable control that can be easily modified.

questions.question: AND gate output is TRUE when:
questions.explanation: AND gate = all inputs must be TRUE (1). Like switches in series — ALL must be closed.

questions.question: OR gate output is TRUE when:
questions.explanation: OR gate = any input TRUE makes output TRUE. Like switches in parallel — any one path works.

questions.question: A NOT gate:
questions.explanation: NOT gate (inverter) flips the input: 1→0 and 0→1.

questions.question: CAD stands for:
questions.explanation: Computer-Aided Design — software for precision 2D/3D technical drawings.

questions.question: Fiber optic advantages:
questions.explanation: Fiber uses light — immune to EMI, supports very high speeds over long distances.

questions.question: PLC scan cycle order:
options: Read Inputs → Execute Program → Update Outputs
questions.explanation: PLC scan: 1) Read all inputs, 2) Execute program logic, 3) Update all outputs, 4) Housekeeping.

questions.question: An HMI is:
options: A user interface for machine operators
questions.explanation: Human-Machine Interface — touchscreen panels displaying real-time data, alarms, and operator controls.

questions.question: SCADA systems:
questions.explanation: SCADA = Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition — centralized monitoring of distributed industrial processes.

questions.question: A network switch:
questions.explanation: Switches direct traffic to specific devices within a network. Routers connect different networks.

questions.question: Binary 1010 in decimal is:
Closed-loop control uses:
questions.explanation: Closed-loop compares actual output to desired setpoint and adjusts automatically — like a thermostat.

questions.question: PID stands for:
questions.explanation: PID control: P responds to current error, I to accumulated error, D to rate of change. Used for precise process control.

questions.question: A servo motor differs from a standard motor by having:
questions.explanation: Servo motors include encoders for precise position/speed feedback, enabling closed-loop motion control.

questions.question: Cat6 Ethernet cable maximum run length is:
questions.explanation: Ethernet cable maximum length is 100 meters (328 feet) per segment for both Cat5e and Cat6.

questions.question: What is the purpose of machine guarding?
options: To protect operators from hazards like rotating parts
questions.explanation: Machine guards protect operators from rotating parts, nip points, flying chips, and sparks as required by OSHA.

questions.question: A confined space requires:
questions.explanation: Confined spaces (tanks, silos, vaults) require permit-required entry with atmospheric testing, ventilation, and rescue plans.

questions.question: If a 20-tooth sprocket at 1200 RPM drives a 40-tooth sprocket, output RPM is:
questions.explanation: RPM_out = RPM_in × (Teeth_driver/Teeth_driven) = 1200 × (20/40) = 600 RPM.

questions.question: A plain (sleeve/journal) bearing is best for:
options: Low speed, high load, or oscillating motion
questions.explanation: Plain bearings use sliding contact — ideal for low speed, high load, or oscillating motion with proper lubrication.

questions.question: Over-greasing a bearing causes:
questions.explanation: Excess grease is churned by the rolling elements, generating heat that can damage the bearing. Proper quantity is critical.

questions.question: An angular misalignment causes vibration primarily in the:
questions.explanation: Angular misalignment (shafts at an angle) produces a dominant twice-per-revolution vibration in the axial direction.

questions.question: An RTD measures temperature using:
questions.explanation: RTDs (Pt100 most common) measure temperature through predictable resistance changes in platinum wire — more accurate than thermocouples.

questions.question: A strain gauge works by:
questions.explanation: Strain gauges change resistance when stretched or compressed — used in load cells and pressure transducers, typically in Wheatstone bridges.

questions.question: A contactor is different from a relay because it:
options: Is rated for switching high-current motor loads
questions.explanation: Contactors are heavy-duty relays rated for higher voltage and current, specifically designed for motor switching.

questions.question: A VFD controls motor speed by varying:
questions.explanation: Variable Frequency Drives control speed by varying both the frequency (which sets speed) and voltage of the power to the motor.

questions.question: An accumulator in a hydraulic system:
questions.explanation: Accumulators store energy to supplement pump flow during peaks, absorb shock, and maintain pressure during brief demand spikes.

questions.question: A sequence valve controls:
questions.explanation: Sequence valves direct flow to a second actuator only after the first reaches set pressure — controlling operation ORDER.

questions.question: A motor nameplate indicates it draws 10 Amps at 120V. Assuming it is purely resistive, what is the power consumption?
questions.explanation: Power (P) = Voltage (V) × Current (I). P = 120V × 10A = 1200 Watts.

questions.question: Three resistors of 10Ω, 20Ω, and 30Ω are connected in series. What is the total resistance?
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, total resistance is the sum of the individual resistors. Rt = 10 + 20 + 30 = 60Ω.

questions.question: Two 100Ω resistors are connected in parallel. What is the total equivalent resistance?
questions.explanation: For two equal resistors in parallel, the total resistance is half the value of one resistor. 100 / 2 = 50Ω.

questions.question: A 480V to 120V step-down transformer has a primary winding of 1000 turns. How many turns are on the secondary winding?
questions.explanation: Turns ratio is proportional to voltage ratio. Vp/Vs = Np/Ns. 480/120 = 4. The ratio is 4:1. Thus, Ns = 1000 / 4 = 250 turns.

questions.question: A hydraulic cylinder has a piston area of 5 square inches. If the system pressure is 1,200 PSI, what is the maximum extension force?
questions.explanation: Force = Pressure × Area. F = 1,200 PSI × 5 sq.in = 6,000 lbs.

questions.question: A hydraulic pump must move an 8,000 lb load using a cylinder with a 4 square inch piston area. How much pressure is required?
questions.explanation: Pressure = Force / Area. P = 8,000 lbs / 4 sq.in = 2,000 PSI.

questions.question: A motor spinning at 1500 RPM has a 4-inch drive pulley connected via a belt to a 12-inch driven pulley. What is the RPM of the driven pulley?
questions.explanation: Speed Ratio = Driven RPM / Driver RPM = Driver Dia / Driven Dia. RPM_driven = 1500 × (4 / 12) = 500 RPM.

questions.question: A gear train consists of a 10-tooth driving gear and a 50-tooth driven gear. What is the gear ratio and torque effect?
questions.explanation: Gear ratio = Driven/Driver = 50/10 = 5:1. Because output speed is reduced, output torque is multiplied by the same factor (5x).

questions.question: A worker needs to set up a ladder to reach a roof that is 24 feet high. According to the OSHA 4:1 rule, how far from the wall should the base of the ladder be placed?
questions.explanation: The 4:1 rule states that the base should be out 1 foot for every 4 feet of height. 24 / 4 = 6 feet.

questions.question: A drawing specifies a dimension of 2.500" ± 0.015". What is the total tolerance allowed?
questions.explanation: Tolerance is the difference between the max limit and min limit. Total tolerance = (+0.015) + (-0.015) = 0.030".

questions.question: An AC circuit operates at 60 Hz. What is the duration (period) of one complete cycle?
questions.explanation: Period (T) = 1 / Frequency (f). T = 1 / 60 = 0.01666... seconds, which is 16.7 milliseconds.

questions.question: A temperature sensor has a 4-20mA output range corresponding to 0°C to 100°C. If the sensor is reading 50°C, what should the expected output current be?
questions.explanation: The span is 16mA (from 4 to 20). 50°C is 50% of the temperature range. 50% of the 16mA span is 8mA. Added to the 4mA baseline, total is 12 mA.

questions.question: What is the decimal equivalent of the 4-bit binary number 1101?
A 4-pole AC induction motor operating on a 60 Hz power supply has a synchronous speed of:
If you apply 24V DC to a circuit with a 100Ω and a 200Ω resistor in series, what is the current flowing through the circuit?
questions.explanation: First, find total resistance: Rt = 100 + 200 = 300Ω. Then apply Ohm's Law: I = V / R = 24 / 300 = 0.08 A.

questions.question: Which logic gate outputs a '1' ONLY when both Input A AND Input B are '0'?
questions.explanation: A NOR gate is an OR gate followed by an inverter. It only outputs a '1' (TRUE) when all of its inputs are '0' (FALSE).

questions.question: A pneumatic cylinder has a 2-inch diameter. Approximately what is the surface area of the piston?
questions.explanation: Radius = Diameter/2 = 1 inch. Area = π·r² = 3.14159 × (1)² ≈ 3.14 sq.in.

questions.question: If a hydraulic pump produces a flow rate of 10 GPM (gallons per minute) to a cylinder, increasing the flow rate to 20 GPM will:
options: Cause the cylinder to lock up
questions.explanation: Fluid flow rate determines actuator velocity (speed). Doubling the flow rate will double the speed. Pressure determines force.

questions.question: If a dial indicator completes exactly 2 full revolutions on its main dial (which indicates 0.100 inch per revolution), what distance has the spindle moved?
questions.explanation: Each revolution equals 0.100 inch displacement. Two complete revolutions means 2 × 0.100 = 0.200 inch.

questions.question: To properly check mechanical runout on a motor shaft, what tool is primarily used?
questions.explanation: A dial indicator is the standard tool to measure runout, endplay, and alignment. It detects the precise deviation of a surface as it rotates.

questions.question: In a 3-phase, 480V motor branch circuit, which device provides protection exclusively against motor OVERLOAD (heat/thermal accumulation)?
questions.explanation: The overload relay senses motor current and trips when current exceeds the safe thermal profile of the motor, protecting against overloads, not instantaneous short circuits.

questions.question: Which network topology consists of a central switch or hub that connects out to all individual devices?
questions.explanation: A Star topology uses a central networking unit (like an Ethernet switch) where each node connects individually to the central hub/switch.

questions.question: A worker is drilling hole in a piece of steel plate on a drill press. Which action is the most critical for safety?
options: Wearing gloves to prevent chips from hitting hands
Clamping the workpiece securely to the drill press table
Increasing drill speed as it breaks through
Applying oil with a bare hand
questions.explanation: The workpiece MUST be securely clamped. If a drill bit catches, a loose piece of sheet metal will act as a spinning blade ('helicoptering') and cause severe injury. Gloves should generally NOT be worn around rotating spindles due to entanglement risk.

questions.question: What happens when an NPN transistor has a positive voltage applied to its base relative to its emitter?
options: It blocks current from collector to emitter
It conducts current from collector to emitter
questions.explanation: An NPN transistor turns ON (conducts current from the collector to the emitter) when a positive bias voltage is applied to the base, acting as a switch.

questions.question: A 100 pound object is lifted using a Class 1 lever. The distance from the fulcrum to the effort is 4 feet, and the distance from the fulcrum to the load is 1 foot. What is the effort force required?
questions.explanation: Mechanical Advantage = Effort Arm / Load Arm = 4ft / 1ft = 4.  Effort Force = Load / MA = 100 lbs / 4 = 25 lbs.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the correct procedure for measuring current with an ammeter?
options: Connect it in parallel with the power source
Connect it in series with the load being measured
Connect it across the open switch
It does not matter how it is connected
questions.explanation: Ammeters must be connected in series with the component to measure the flow of current running through the circuit. Connecting in parallel can cause a short circuit and blow the meter's fuse.

questions.question: When interpreting MSDS/SDS sheets, which section provides details about hazardous ingredients?
questions.explanation: Section 3 covers the Composition/Information on Ingredients, which includes chemical names, common names, and CAS numbers.

questions.question: In pneumatic systems, which valve accurately determines the speed at which a cylinder extends?
questions.explanation: A flow control (or needle) valve regulates the volume of air entering or leaving the cylinder, directly dictating its speed.

questions.question: What is the function of a limit switch in an automated system?
options: To limit the voltage in the power supply
To detect the physical presence or position of an object
questions.explanation: A limit switch uses a physical lever, plunger, or roller that triggers electrical contacts when an object physically hits it, detecting position or end-of-travel.

questions.question: To properly calibrate electrical equipment, a technician should:
options: Hit it. If it works, it's calibrated.
Compare its measurements to a known traceable standard.
questions.explanation: Calibration is the process of adjusting an instrument to match a verifiable, traceable standard (like NIST) to guarantee accuracy.

questions.question: A microprocessor in a modern control system acts as the:
questions.explanation: The microprocessor is the central processing unit (CPU) that handles truth tables, logic gates, and calculation instructions.

questions.question: When selecting wire sizes per NEC rules, what dictates the required AWG gauge?
options: The maximum amperage load of the circuit
The physical length of the conduit
questions.explanation: Wire size (gauge) must be chosen based on the maximum current (amperage load). Lower AWG numbers carry higher amperage.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a potential energy hazard?
questions.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy waiting to be released. Compressed springs, suspended loads, and pressurized vessels are lethal potential energy hazards.

questions.question: Plain bearings (bushings) are typically chosen over roller bearings when the application involves:
options: Low speed and extremely high loads
questions.explanation: Plain bearings have a massive surface area of sliding contact, allowing them to support massive loads at slow or oscillating speeds. They require thick lubrication films.

questions.question: When measuring the shaft alignment of two coupled motors, you would most likely use a:
questions.explanation: Dial indicators (and laser alignment tools) offer thousandths-of-an-inch precision to check radial or axial runout during shaft alignment.

questions.question: A truth table for an AND gate with two inputs will only output TRUE (1) if:
options: Input A is 1 and Input B is 0
Input A is 0 and Input B is 1
Both Input A and Input B are 1
Both Input A and Input B are 0
questions.explanation: AND logic requires ALL conditions to be met. Therefore, both input A AND input B must be TRUE (1).

questions.question: Which fastener prevents heavy machinery nuts from loosening due to intense vibration?
options: A split lock washer or threadlocker fluid
A cotter pin in a straight bolt
questions.explanation: Split ring lock washers, nylon-insert nuts, and threadlocker adhesives (like Loctite) actively push against threads to prevent back-out from vibration.

questions.question: To perform proper machine guarding of a chain and sprocket drive, the guard should:
options: Completely enclose the nip points and rotating elements
Be easily removable without tools while running
questions.explanation: OSHA requires all nip points and rotating mechanical drives to be strictly enclosed so hands/clothing cannot get caught. Guards should require a tool for removal.

questions.question: When converting from Imperial to Metric measurements, 2 inches is equal to:
options: Both A and C are correct
questions.explanation: 1 inch = 25.4 mm. Therefore, 2 inches = 50.8 mm, which is exactly equal to 5.08 cm.

questions.question: Proper application of a multimeter's Ohmmeter function requires that the component being tested:
options: Has an AC voltage, not DC
Is completely disconnected from all electrical power
questions.explanation: Ohmmeters push a tiny internal battery voltage through the component to measure resistance. If the circuit is powered, it will destroy the meter or give false readings.

questions.question: Which of these pairs represents components connected in PARALLEL?
options: Current flows through one to reach the other
If one fails, the entire circuit stops
Voltage is the same across both components
Total resistance increases when more components are added
questions.explanation: In parallel circuits, all branches connect to the same source nodes, meaning the voltage drop across all parallel branches is identical.

questions.question: When working with PLCs, the abbreviation CAD most generally refers to:
questions.explanation: Computer-Aided Design software is used globally in engineering to create 2D electrical schematics and 3D mechanical models.

questions.question: In a pneumatic piping system, the primary function of a "filter" in an FRL unit is to:
options: Regulate the pressure down to 80 PSI
Remove solid particulates and liquid water drops from compressed air
questions.explanation: The Filter physically scrubs solids and drops out moisture/water using centrifugal force and micron screens to protect downstream valves and cylinders.

questions.question: To correctly use a torque wrench, the technician must:
options: Jerk the wrench quickly until it clicks
Pull with a smooth, steady continuous motion until the specified setting is reached
Use an extension pipe for more leverage
questions.explanation: Torque wrenches measure rotational force accurately only when applied with slow, steady, 90-degree pulling pressure. Jerking or using 'cheater bars' voids the calibration.

questions.question: In the event of a workplace chemical spill, where should a technician look first to determine safe clean-up procedures?
questions.explanation: The SDS (Safety Data Sheet) has specific sections on how to safely handle spills, fires, and exposures regarding the exact chemical involved.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered one of the major engineering fields?
questions.explanation: Major engineering fields include Civil, Mechanical, Electrical, Aerospace, Structural, and Chemical engineering.

questions.question: What method generates a three-view drawing (top, front, side) of an object?
questions.explanation: Orthographic projection places a theoretical plane between the observer and the object to extract distinct 2D views (top, front, side).

questions.question: Which of the following are considered core concepts of technology?
options: Skillful research, ethical behavior, and critical thinking
questions.explanation: Skillful research, ethical behavior, and critical thinking form the foundational core concepts for advancing technology properly.

questions.question: Which option lists important milestones in the progression of the engineering field?
options: Invention of the internet, the steam engine, the wheel
Henry Ford's assembly line, Wright brothers' first flight, Thomas Edison's light bulb
The discovery of fire and bronze tools
The printing press and the telegraph
questions.explanation: Ford's assembly line, the Wright brothers' flight, Edison's mass-produced light bulb, and Goddard's liquid fuel rockets are major documented milestones.

questions.question: What is the very first step in the engineering design process?
options: Identify the need or define the problem
questions.explanation: The engineering design process always begins with step 1: Stating the problem or identifying the need.

questions.question: When analyzing and choosing a solution, which of the following is evaluated to identify strengths and weaknesses?
questions.explanation: Evaluating a solution requires assessing Efficiency, Cost, Safety, Functionality, Durability, and Material Availability.

questions.question: Which machining process is primarily used to turn a rotating cylindrical workpiece against a cutting tool?
